narrative: As an Asian male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.50—above the typical 3.21 average—and a competitive LSAT score of 37, slightly edging out the average of 36.70, you brought several strengths to your law school application. Coming from a high family income bracket also positioned you favorably in some respects. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated an 80.3% chance that you would fail it, leading directly to the rejection of your application. This high predicted failure risk means the school views you as a student who, based on patterns from past applicants, might struggle to meet the bar despite your promise. The decision hinged most heavily on your academic decile rankings from earlier years in school: your Year 3 decile of 2—well below the average of about 5.5—stood out as the strongest factor pushing the prediction toward failure, suggesting your performance that year raised red flags compared to peers who succeeded on the bar. Your Year 1 decile of 1, also much lower than the typical 5.7, reinforced this concern as the second-biggest influence, indicating early academic challenges that the model links to bar exam struggles. On a more positive note, your high family income was the third-most influential factor, nudging the prediction slightly toward success, likely because students from similar backgrounds have historically shown better bar passage rates. Overall, these early decile rankings outweighed your stronger recent metrics like GPA and LSAT, placing you below the profile of typical admitted students who tend to have consistently higher school deciles alongside good test scores. Your full-time student status aligns with the vast majority at 92.7%, so it didn't sway things much either way. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your school deciles, as improving those could dramatically shift future predictions—many students in your position have succeeded by addressing early weaknesses head-on. You might also seek targeted bar prep resources tailored for Asian American applicants, who sometimes face unique pressures, and reapply after demonstrating upward trends in academics. With your LSAT and GPA as anchors, you're not far off, and persistence here could open doors.
